title: The Seventeenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: September 27, 2020

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Seventeenth Sunday after Pentecost falls in Ordinary Time, the long green season of the church calendar following Pentecost. The RCL readings for this Sunday were likely Exodus 17:1-7 (water from the rock), Psalm 78:1-4, 12-16, Philippians 2:1-13 (Christ's humility and exaltation), and Matthew 21:23-32 (the parable of two sons). This sermon would have occurred during the traditional post-Labor Day return to full liturgical programming after summer schedules.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of processing the July 2020 General Convention vote (held virtually due to COVID-19) that approved the removal of the Confederate battle flag from the church seal and various reparations resolutions. Bishop Rob Faire of the Diocese of Colorado was serving during what many described as a tumultuous period for the denomination regarding racial justice and institutional reform. The broader Anglican Communion was navigating deep divisions over sexuality and biblical interpretation, though these debates were less prominently visible in September 2020 given the pandemic's dominance.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the ninth month of COVID-19 pandemic response with ongoing debate over school reopening protocols and mask mandates. The week of September 20-27 saw Colorado's confirmed COVID cases continuing to rise, with the state averaging over 400 new cases daily. Weather-wise, early fall had arrived with cooling temperatures after an extremely active summer fire season; the Cameron Peak Fire (Colorado's largest on record) was still actively burning in Larimer County northwest of Denver, though it had been burning since August 13 and was in late stages.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The presidential election was six weeks away with President Trump and former Vice President Joe Biden in intense campaign mode; Trump held a rally in Nevada on September 24-25, continuing his "law and order" messaging. The Supreme Court confirmation hearings for Amy Coney Barrett were scheduled to begin September 28, dominating political discourse with questions about abortion and the Affordable Care Act. The pandemic remained the dominant national story with over 7.5 million confirmed US cases and nearly 210,000 deaths; the racial justice protests that intensified after George Floyd's May death were ongoing but showing less mainstream media focus by late September.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Nations General Assembly was meeting virtually in New York with world leaders addressing the pandemic and its economic impacts; Brazil's Jair Bolsonaro and India's Narendra Modi were among those speaking. The Russian opposition politician Alexei Navalny had been poisoned (incident in August) and was recovering in a Berlin hospital, creating international tensions between Russia and the West. The second wave of COVID-19 was surging across Europe with France and Spain reporting record daily case counts.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was anxious, polarized, and pandemic-fatigued six months into lockdowns and social restrictions. People were navigating simultaneous crises: COVID-19, economic uncertainty, racial reckoning following the summer's protests, and an intensifying presidential election. The cultural default was to conflict—over masks, schools, protests, the Supreme Court—with relatively little shared ground across ideological lines. Sports had cautiously resumed (NFL season had just begun September 10), offering some normalcy, though many Americans remained isolated and struggling with mental health impacts of the prolonged crisis.
